items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Clonmacnoise
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* the heartlands
*County:* County Offaly
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 6th century
*Coords:* 53.32388889, -7.99111111
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clonmacnoise
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Clonmacnoise/@53.3259,-7.9858,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Clonmacnoise_6.jpg/330px-Clonmacnoise_6.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Clonmacnoise or Clonmacnois is a ruined monastery in County Offaly in Ireland on the River Shannon south of Athlone, founded in 544 by Saint Ciarán, from Rathcroghan, County Roscommon. Until the 9th century it had close associations with the kings of Connacht.

_Listing 6 of 10_

listing.desc: Clonmacnoise or Clonmacnois is a ruined monastery in County Offaly in Ireland on the River Shannon south of Athlone, founded in 544 by Saint Ciarán, from Rathcroghan, County Roscommon. Until the 9th century it had close associations with the kings of Connacht.

*Description:*
Ballyfin is a village and parish in the Slieve Bloom Mountains in County Laois, Ireland. It is around 8 km west of Portlaoise, on the L21121 local road which joins the R423 regional road between Mountrath and Mountmellick.

_Listing 7 of 10_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Chesters Roman Fort
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Northumberland
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 2nd century AD
*Coords:* 55.026, -2.139
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cilurnum
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Chesters%20Roman%20Fort/@55.0283,-2.135,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/The_Commanding_Officer%27s_House_%28praetorium%29%2C_AD_150-400%2C_Chesters_Roman_Fort_%28Cilurnum%29%2C_Hadrian%27s_Wall_%2844036170984%29.jpg/330px-The_Commanding_Officer%27s_House_%28praetorium%29%2C_AD_150-400%2C_Chesters_Roman_Fort_%28Cilurnum%29%2C_Hadrian%27s_Wall_%2844036170984%29.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Cilurnum or Cilurvum was an ancient Roman fort on Hadrian's Wall at Chesters near the village of Walwick, Northumberland. It is also known as Walwick Chesters to distinguish it from Great Chesters fort and Halton Chesters.

_Listing 8 of 10_

listing.desc: Cilurnum or Cilurvum was an ancient Roman fort on Hadrian's Wall at Chesters near the village of Walwick, Northumberland. It is also known as Walwick Chesters to distinguish it from Great Chesters fort and Halton Chesters.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Hardknott Roman Fort
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Cumbria
*Type:* castle
*Period:* Roman (2nd century)
*Coords:* 54.40277778, -3.20527778
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hardknott_Roman_Fort
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Hardknott%20Roman%20Fort/@54.4017,-3.2017,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZPFiWhSPF9x2FLXMFZ2sy_7hkekjU2GxsuL1_sdly4GtY0Hhv_1Uqq9xW4uL2OTigjChOYWWLH3gBGuWfKWHvnr0Rr8YnIUNy_ESA2A3ItHpxwbTes6q5INujlHNnkTIKUr0rKdBn2huvhqwg4=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Hardknott Roman Fort is an archeological site, the remains of the Roman fort Mediobogdum, located on the western side of the challenging Hardknott Pass in the English Lake District. The fort was built between 120 and 138 on a rocky spur, and was initially garrisoned by a detachment of the Cohors IV Delmatarum from the Dalmatian coast. It was abandoned around a decade later, then reoccupied circa 200 and remained in use for much of the next two centuries.

_Listing 9 of 10_

listing.name: Hardknott Roman Fort
listing.desc: Hardknott Roman Fort is an archeological site, the remains of the Roman fort Mediobogdum, located on the western side of the challenging Hardknott Pass in the English Lake District. The fort was built between 120 and 138 on a rocky spur, and was initially garrisoned by a detachment of the Cohors IV Delmatarum from the Dalmatian coast. It was abandoned around a decade later, then reoccupied circa 200 and remained in use for much of the next two centuries.

Hardknott Roman fort, bath-house, parade ground and tribunal, 4 Roman roads, Roman quarries and 3 cairns

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Sycamore Gap
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Northumberland
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 2nd century AD
*Coords:* 55.0036, -2.3739
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sycamore_Gap_tree
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Sycamore%20Gap/@55.0017,-2.3617,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMGY2v5jmOb01BISJK63v3TPFNB4hR8288s-eKRuS9arZP6M6fx3pYQtf9cGr_yUfDQPsTccsYAPpypqwFJj8z03gffiwsh9gAiIkQ1tsWn7QBuNHfP2dtWisgwywn3eo6r6GCEbIVuOMknFQ=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
The Sycamore Gap tree, also known as the Robin Hood tree, was a 100–120-year-old sycamore tree next to Hadrian's Wall near Crag Lough in Northumberland, England. It was illegally felled in 2023 by Daniel Graham and Adam Carruthers, but has since sprouted from the stump. Standing in a dramatic dip in the landscape created by glacial meltwater, it was one of the country's most photographed trees and an emblem for the North East of England. It derived its alternative name from featuring in a prominent scene in the 1991 film Robin Hood: Prince of Thieves. The tree won the 2016 England Tree of the Year award, part of the Woodland Trust's annual competition that celebrates culturally and environmentally significant trees across the UK. As the national winner for England, the Sycamore Gap tree received a £1,000 care grant funded by the People's Postcode Lottery.

_Listing 10 of 10_
